description: Windows Security Event IDs - critical SOC subset (~120 events)

events.description: Audit log cleared - anti-forensics indicator. Clearing Security log requires SeSecurityPrivilege. Always investigate who cleared and why. Correlate with preceding events that attacker may be hiding.
Windows starting up (lsass.exe started). Baseline event for system boot timeline. Unexpected restarts may indicate crash-based exploitation or attacker reboot after persistence install.
Authentication package loaded by LSA. Monitor for non-standard packages (e.g., mimilib.dll loaded as SSP for credential harvesting). Compare against known-good baseline.

events.event_name: Trusted logon process registered with LSA
events.description: Trusted logon process registered with LSA. Unusual processes registering with LSA may indicate credential interception. Should only be standard Windows components.

events.event_name: Security Account Manager notification package loaded
events.description: Security Account Manager notification package loaded. SAM notification packages see plaintext passwords during changes. Attacker-installed packages (e.g., mimilib) capture credentials.
System time changed. Time manipulation can disrupt log correlation and evade time-based detections. Monitor for changes outside NTP sync patterns.
Security package loaded by LSA. New security packages loaded at boot. Non-default packages may indicate credential interception tools installed as SSPs.

Successful logon - check LogonType (2=interactive, 3=network, 4=batch, 5=service, 7=unlock, 8=network cleartext, 9=newcredentials, 10=RDP, 11=cached). LogonType 3 from unusual hosts indicates lateral movement. LogonType 10 from external IPs indicates RDP brute force success. LogonType 9 is runas /netonly.

Failed logon attempt - high volume from single source indicates brute force. Status codes: 0xC000006D=bad username/password, 0xC0000234=account locked, 0xC0000072=account disabled, 0xC000006A=correct username wrong password. SubStatus provides granularity.
User/device claims information. Provides claim-based authorization context. Useful for tracking dynamic access control decisions in sensitive environments.
Group membership information. Logged with 4624, shows all groups for the authenticated user. Useful for identifying over-privileged accounts and unexpected group memberships.
Logoff (account logged off). Pair with 4624 using Logon ID to calculate session duration. Short sessions from service accounts may indicate automated tool usage.
User initiated logoff. Unlike 4634, indicates deliberate user action. Useful for tracking user session patterns and identifying compromised interactive sessions.

events.event_name: Logon using explicit credentials (runas / pass-the-hash indicator)
events.description: Logon using explicit credentials (runas) - indicates credential use outside normal context. Common in pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, and lateral movement. Monitor for service accounts used interactively or unusual source-target account pairs.
Replay attack detected. Kerberos detected a replay attempt. May indicate credential theft and replay-based attacks. Always investigate, very low false-positive rate.
Handle to object requested. High volume event when object access auditing enabled. Filter by ObjectType and AccessMask for meaningful detections. Key for file/registry access monitoring.
Registry value modified. Tracks registry changes when SACL is set. Monitor Run/RunOnce keys, Services, Image File Execution Options for persistence and tampering.
Handle to object closed. Pair with 4656 for complete object access timeline. Useful for session correlation but high volume.
Object deleted. Tracks deletion of audited objects. Monitor for mass deletion patterns (ransomware) or deletion of security tools and logs.
Handle to object requested (SAM). Access to Security Account Manager objects. Monitor for enumeration of domain users/groups which indicates reconnaissance.
Operation performed on AD DS object. Tracks Active Directory changes. Monitor for DCSync (Replicating Directory Changes permission), AdminSDHolder modifications, and GPO changes.
Attempt to access object (file/folder/registry). Requires SACL on object. Monitor for access to sensitive files (SAM, NTDS.dit, SYSTEM hive) and bulk file access patterns (data exfiltration).
Permissions on object changed. DACL modifications on files, registry, or AD objects. Attackers weaken permissions for persistence or to access restricted resources.

events.event_name: Special privileges assigned to new logon (admin logon)
events.description: Special privileges assigned to new logon - SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, SeRestorePrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege assigned indicates admin-level logon. Monitor for unexpected privilege assignments, especially to non-admin accounts.
Privileged service called. Tracks use of sensitive privileges like SeSystemEnvironmentPrivilege (UEFI vars) and SeLoadDriverPrivilege (kernel drivers). Monitor for privilege abuse.
Operation attempted on privileged object. Tracks privileged operations on secured objects. Useful for detecting unauthorized access to high-value targets.

events.event_name: New process created (with command line if enabled)
events.description: New process created - requires command line logging (GPO: Include command line in process creation events). Critical for detecting LOLBin abuse (certutil, mshta, regsvr32, rundll32), encoded PowerShell (-enc/-e), suspicious parent-child chains (winword->cmd->powershell), and living-off-the-land techniques.
Process exited. Pair with 4688 for process lifetime analysis. Very short-lived processes may indicate reconnaissance commands or download cradles.
Attempt to duplicate handle. Handle duplication between processes. Used in some injection techniques and privilege escalation methods.

events.event_name: Backup of DPAPI master key attempted
events.description: Backup of DPAPI master key attempted. DPAPI keys protect user secrets (browser passwords, credential manager). Backup attempts may indicate credential harvesting (mimikatz dpapi module).

events.event_name: Recovery of DPAPI master key attempted
events.description: Recovery of DPAPI master key attempted. Recovering DPAPI keys allows decryption of all user secrets. Monitor for recovery by non-system accounts, indicates active credential theft.

events.event_name: Protection of auditable protected data attempted
events.description: Protection of auditable protected data attempted. Encryption of data using DPAPI. Unusual patterns may indicate ransomware or data protection evasion.

events.event_name: Unprotection of auditable protected data attempted
events.description: Unprotection of auditable protected data attempted. Decryption of DPAPI-protected data. Bulk unprotection may indicate credential harvesting from browser stores.
Primary token assigned to process. Token manipulation can enable privilege escalation. Monitor for token assignment to unexpected processes.

Service installed on system - persistence mechanism. Check for services with unusual binary paths (temp dirs, encoded commands), services running as SYSTEM with user-writable binaries, and services created by non-admin tools.

Scheduled task created - persistence/execution mechanism. Check task actions for encoded commands, unusual binaries, remote task creation (lateral movement via at/schtasks), and tasks running as SYSTEM or another user.
Scheduled task deleted. Deletion after execution is a cleanup technique. Monitor for create-execute-delete patterns indicating one-shot persistence.
Scheduled task enabled. Re-enabling previously disabled tasks may indicate reactivated persistence. Correlate with task content from 4698.
Scheduled task disabled. Disabling security-related tasks (AV scans, log collection) indicates defense evasion.
Scheduled task updated. Modification of existing tasks can hijack legitimate scheduled operations. Monitor for changes to action, trigger, or principal.
Token right adjusted. Tracks privilege enable/disable on tokens. Enabling SeDebugPrivilege at runtime is a strong indicator of credential dumping or injection.
User right assigned. New privilege granted to user/group. Monitor for SeTcbPrivilege, SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege grants outside normal admin operations.
User right removed. Privilege removal may indicate cleanup after privilege escalation or legitimate hardening.
Trust to domain created. New domain trust creation is a high-impact AD change. Unauthorized trusts enable cross-domain attacks and persistence.
Trust to domain removed. Trust removal is major infrastructure change. May indicate response to breach or unauthorized configuration change.
Kerberos policy changed. Changes to Kerberos ticket lifetime, renewal, or encryption settings. Weakening encryption types enables downgrade attacks.
Trusted domain information modified. Changes to existing trust relationships. Monitor for trust direction changes or SID filtering modifications.
System security access granted. Account granted system-level logon rights. Monitor for SeInteractiveLogonRight, SeRemoteInteractiveLogonRight grants to unexpected accounts.
System security access removed. Logon rights removed. May indicate hardening or attacker covering tracks after using temporary access.
System audit policy changed. Audit policy modification is a defense evasion technique. Disabling auditing for specific categories hides attacker activity. Always investigate.

User account created - check for accounts created outside IAM process, especially during off-hours, from unusual source workstations, or with names mimicking service accounts. Common persistence technique.
User account enabled. Re-enabling disabled accounts provides access without creating new detectable accounts. Monitor for enabling of previously disabled service accounts.
Password change attempted. Users change their own password. High failure rate may indicate password policy testing by attacker with partial credentials.
Password reset attempted. Admin-initiated password reset. Monitor for resets of high-privilege accounts, especially from non-helpdesk systems.
User account disabled. Disabling accounts may be defensive (compromise response) or offensive (denial of service to legitimate admins during attack).
User account deleted. Account deletion destroys audit trail for that identity. Attackers may delete accounts created for persistence during cleanup.
Security-enabled global group created. New AD security groups may be created for unauthorized access delegation. Monitor for groups created outside change management.

events.event_name: Member added to security-enabled global group
events.description: Member added to security-enabled global group. Adding users to privileged groups (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins) is a primary privilege escalation vector.

events.event_name: Member removed from security-enabled global group
events.description: Member removed from security-enabled global group. Removal may indicate cleanup after escalation or defensive response. Correlate with prior 4728 events.
Security-enabled global group deleted. Group deletion removes access controls and audit trail. Monitor for deletion of security-critical groups.
Security-enabled local group created. Local security groups on servers/workstations. Monitor for creation on high-value targets.

events.event_name: Member added to security-enabled local group
events.description: Member added to security-enabled local group - adding user to local Administrators group is a common privilege escalation technique. Monitor all additions to Administrators, Remote Desktop Users, and Backup Operators groups.

events.event_name: Member removed from security-enabled local group
events.description: Member removed from security-enabled local group. Removal from privileged local groups may indicate defensive response or attacker cleanup.
Security-enabled local group deleted. Deletion of local security groups on endpoints. May indicate post-exploitation cleanup.
Security-enabled local group changed. Group attribute changes may weaken security settings or change group scope/type.
Security-enabled global group changed. Changes to AD group attributes. Monitor for scope changes that expand access.

User account changed. Tracks modifications to user attributes. Monitor for UAC flag changes (dont-require-preauth enables AS-REP roasting), SPN changes (enables Kerberoasting), and delegation flag changes.
Domain policy changed. Changes to domain-level policies (password policy, lockout policy, Kerberos policy). Weakening policies enables brute force and downgrade attacks.
User account locked out. Lockout indicates brute force against the account. Check source workstation (CallerComputerName) to identify attacking host. High-volume lockouts indicate password spray.
Computer account created. New computer accounts in AD. Unauthorized computer objects may be used for resource-based constrained delegation attacks.
Computer account changed. Monitor for changes to msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity (RBCD attacks) and ServicePrincipalName modifications.
Computer account deleted. Deletion may disrupt services or indicate cleanup of attack infrastructure in AD.
Security-disabled local group created. Distribution groups don't control access but converting them to security groups later can bypass monitoring.
Security-enabled universal group created. Universal groups replicate to all DCs in the forest. Monitor for unauthorized creation.
Security-enabled universal group changed. Changes to forest-wide security groups have broad impact.

events.event_name: Member added to security-enabled universal group
events.description: Member added to security-enabled universal group. Universal group membership changes affect entire forest. Monitor for Enterprise Admins additions.

events.event_name: Member removed from security-enabled universal group
events.description: Member removed from security-enabled universal group. Monitor for unauthorized removals that may weaken security controls.
Group type changed. Converting between security and distribution groups, or changing scope. May enable or circumvent access controls.
SID History added to account. SID History injection enables access to resources of another account. Used in domain escalation and inter-forest attacks. Always investigate.
SID History add attempt failed. Failed SID History injection. Still indicates attack attempt even though it failed.
User account unlocked. Manual unlock after lockout. Verify through helpdesk ticket. Unlock without corresponding request may indicate attacker resetting their brute force target.

Kerberos TGT requested (AS-REQ) - abnormal encryption types (0x17=RC4) may indicate AS-REP roasting when account has dont-require-preauth set. Monitor for TGT requests from unusual hosts or for service accounts from non-service workstations.

Kerberos service ticket requested (TGS-REQ) - encryption type 0x17 (RC4) may indicate Kerberoasting. Monitor for high volume TGS requests from single user to multiple SPNs. Ticket options 0x40810000 is normal, unusual options may indicate forged tickets.
Kerberos service ticket renewed. Frequent renewals or renewals for unusual services may indicate ticket manipulation.

Kerberos pre-authentication failed - Status 0x18=wrong password (brute force), 0x12=account disabled/expired, 0x17=password expired, 0x25=clock skew. High volume from single IP indicates password spray. Single account high volume indicates targeted brute force.
Kerberos ticket request failed. Failed TGS requests may indicate invalid or forged tickets being used. Monitor for patterns suggesting Golden/Silver ticket issues.
Account mapped for logon. Certificate-based authentication mapping. Monitor for unexpected certificate-to-account mappings.

NTLM authentication attempted (domain controller) - legacy protocol, monitor for pass-the-hash. Should be rare in Kerberos environments. ErrorCode 0xC000006A=bad password, 0xC0000064=user not found. High volume indicates credential stuffing.
Session reconnected (RDP reconnect). RDP session reconnection from different client may indicate session hijacking or credential reuse.
Session disconnected (RDP disconnect). Disconnection without logoff leaves session active. Attackers may disconnect to keep sessions available.

events.event_name: ACL set on admin group member accounts
events.description: ACL set on admin group member accounts. Automatic ACL reset on admin accounts. Failures may indicate tampered admin account permissions.
Account name changed. Renaming accounts can disguise compromised or malicious accounts. Monitor for renaming to/from names resembling service accounts.
Password hash accessed. Direct access to password hashes, indicates credential harvesting. Always investigate - may indicate DCSync or SAM database access.
Password Policy Checking API called. Reconnaissance of password policy. Attackers check policy to optimize brute force parameters.
DSRM admin password set attempt. Directory Services Restore Mode password allows offline AD access. Unauthorized changes enable persistent AD backdoor.
User local group membership enumerated. Reconnaissance activity - enumerating who belongs to local groups. Common in BloodHound/SharpHound data collection.
Security-enabled local group membership enumerated. Reconnaissance of local admin group membership. Part of standard post-exploitation enumeration.
Workstation locked. User activity tracking. Unexpected lock events may indicate screensaver policy but generally low detection value.
Workstation unlocked. Tracks when users return to workstations. Useful for timeline reconstruction during investigations.
Screensaver invoked. User activity tracking for timeline purposes.
Screensaver dismissed. User activity tracking for timeline purposes.
RDP access denied (NLA). Network Level Authentication rejection. Indicates RDP access attempt that failed before full connection. Source IP is valuable for perimeter defense.
Boot Configuration Data loaded. Tracks BCD settings at startup. Changes may indicate bootkits or secure boot tampering.

events.event_name: Windows Firewall exception list rule added
events.description: Windows Firewall exception list rule added. New firewall rules may open unauthorized access. Monitor for rules allowing inbound connections to unusual ports.

events.event_name: Windows Firewall exception list rule modified
events.description: Windows Firewall exception list rule modified. Rule modifications may weaken existing firewall protection. Monitor for scope broadening changes.

events.event_name: Windows Firewall exception list rule deleted
events.description: Windows Firewall exception list rule deleted. Rule deletion may remove protection. Less concerning than additions unless security rules are removed.
Windows Firewall setting changed. Profile or global firewall settings modified. Disabling firewall is common defense evasion.

events.event_name: Special groups assigned to new logon
events.description: Special groups assigned to new logon. Custom special group monitoring. Alerts when members of designated groups authenticate. Configured via audit policy.
Windows Firewall Service started. Normal at boot. Mid-session starts may indicate firewall was previously stopped.
Windows Firewall Service stopped. Firewall service stop is defense evasion. Should only occur during planned maintenance. Always investigate unexpected stops.
Windows Firewall blocked application. Application tried to accept connections but was blocked. May indicate malware failing to open C2 listener.

events.event_name: Code integrity determined image hash is not valid
events.description: Code integrity determined image hash is not valid. Unsigned or tampered binary detected. May indicate malware or modified system files. Investigate the image path.
Key file operation. Cryptographic key operations. Monitor for unauthorized access to encryption keys used for data protection.
Key migration operation. Cryptographic key export/migration. May indicate key theft for later decryption of protected data.
Directory service object modified. AD object attribute changes. Critical for detecting AdminSDHolder poisoning, GPO modifications, and schema changes.
Directory service object created. New AD objects. Monitor for computer objects (RBCD), GPOs, and organizational units created outside change management.
Directory service object undeleted. Restoring deleted AD objects. May indicate attacker recovering previously cleaned malicious objects.
Directory service object moved. Moving AD objects between OUs may change inherited permissions and group policies applied to the object.

Network share accessed - monitor for access to ADMIN$, C$, IPC$ from unusual sources indicating lateral movement. Access to SYSVOL and NETLOGON is normal for domain members but unusual patterns may indicate GPO attacks.
Directory service object deleted. AD object deletion. Monitor for deletion of security groups, GPOs, and organizational units.
Network share created. New shares on systems. Attackers create shares for data staging or to facilitate lateral movement.
Network share modified. Share permission changes may weaken access controls on sensitive data.
Network share deleted. Share deletion may indicate cleanup of data staging shares used during exfiltration.

events.event_name: Network share object access checked (detailed file share auditing)
events.description: Network share object access checked (detailed file share auditing) - granular share access with access mask. Useful for detecting ransomware file enumeration patterns, mass file reads (exfiltration), and unauthorized access to sensitive shares.
Windows Filtering Platform blocked packet. WFP packet block events. High volume from single source may indicate port scanning or malware communication attempts.

events.event_name: WFP permitted application to listen on port
events.description: WFP permitted application to listen on port. New listening ports opened. Monitor for unusual applications binding to ports (backdoors, C2 listeners).
WFP permitted connection. Allowed network connections. Useful for network traffic analysis when full packet capture unavailable.
WFP blocked connection. Blocked outbound or inbound connections. Failed C2 communication or blocked lateral movement attempts.

events.event_name: WFP permitted bind to local port
events.description: WFP permitted bind to local port. Application allowed to bind a port. Monitor for unusual binaries or unexpected port numbers.

events.event_name: WFP blocked bind to local port
events.description: WFP blocked bind to local port. Application blocked from binding port. May indicate malware prevented from opening listener.
Credential Manager credentials backed up. Backup of stored credentials may indicate credential harvesting for offline extraction.

events.event_name: Credential Manager credentials restored from backup
events.description: Credential Manager credentials restored from backup. Restoring credentials from backup. May indicate credential injection or migration from compromised host.

events.event_name: Requested credentials delegation disallowed by policy
events.description: Requested credentials delegation disallowed by policy. CredSSP delegation blocked. May indicate attack attempt using credential delegation.
Credential Manager credentials read. Reading stored credentials. Tools like mimikatz read credential manager for saved passwords. Monitor for non-browser processes reading credentials.
Vault Find Credential. Vault enumeration of stored credentials. Part of credential harvesting workflow.
Vault credentials read. Direct read of Windows Vault credentials. Credential theft indicator when performed by non-standard processes.
Vault credentials read. Additional vault credential read event. Same detection context as 5381.
New external device recognized. USB/external device connection. Monitor for unauthorized removable media in sensitive environments. Data exfiltration vector.

events.event_name: New service installed (System log, not Security)
events.description: New service installed (System log) - persistence mechanism. Correlate with 4697 for full service installation picture. Check ServiceFileName for encoded commands, paths to temp directories, or unusual executables. ServiceType kernel driver (0x1) is highest risk.

events.event_name: NTLM authentication to domain controller (Sysmon-like, AppLocker in some configs)
events.description: NTLM authentication to domain controller. NTLM pass-through authentication. Should be minimal in modern Kerberos environments. High volume indicates misconfigured applications or pass-the-hash activity.
